subject: Physics
q: An electron in a hydrogen atom revolves around its nucleus with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>76</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in an orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>. The magnetic field produced at the nucleus of the hydrogen atom is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>.
question: An electron in a hydrogen atom revolves around its nucleus with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>76</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in an orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math>. The magnetic field produced at the nucleus of the hydrogen atom is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>.
solution:
The magnetic field due to the electron is given by 

B=μ0qvsin90°4πr2

The given data is 

v=6.76×106 m s-1r=0.52×10-10 m

Thus, the magnetic field is 

B=10-7×1.6××10-19×6.76×1060.52×0.52×10-20=40 T

subject: Physics
q: A charged particle with charge $\mathrm{q}$ enters a region of constant, uniform and mutually orthogonal fields $\mathrm{\vec{E}}$ and $\mathrm{\vec{B}}$ with a velocity $\mathrm{\vec{v}}$ perpendicular to both $\mathrm{\vec{E}}$ and $\mathrm{\vec{B}}$, and comes out without any change in magnitude or direction of $\mathrm{\vec{v}}$. Then
question: A charged particle with charge $\mathrm{q}$ enters a region of constant, uniform and mutually orthogonal fields $\mathrm{\vec{E}}$ and $\mathrm{\vec{B}}$ with a velocity $\mathrm{\vec{v}}$ perpendicular to both $\mathrm{\vec{E}}$ and $\mathrm{\vec{B}}$, and comes out without any change in magnitude or direction of $\mathrm{\vec{v}}$. Then
solution: $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$

subject: Physics
q: The magnitude of the magnetic field $(B)$ due to loop $A B C D$ at the origin $(O)$ is
question: The magnitude of the magnetic field $(B)$ due to loop $A B C D$ at the origin $(O)$ is
solution: Net magnetic field due to loop $A B C D$ at $O$ is $$ \begin{aligned} & B=B_{A B}+B_{B C}+B_{C D}+B_{D A} \\ & =0+\frac{\mu_o I}{4 \pi a} \times \frac{\pi}{6}+0-\frac{\mu_o I}{4 \pi b} \times \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\mu_o I}{24 a}-\frac{\mu_o I}{24 b}=\frac{\mu_o I}{24 a b}(b-a) \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q:
A straight magnetic strip has a magnetic moment of $44 \mathrm{Am}^2$. If the strip is bent in a semicircular shape, its magnetic moment will be _______ $\mathrm{Am}^2$.
(given $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ )
question:
A straight magnetic strip has a magnetic moment of $44 \mathrm{Am}^2$. If the strip is bent in a semicircular shape, its magnetic moment will be _______ $\mathrm{Am}^2$.
(given $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ )
solution: Magnetic moment of straight wire $=\mathrm{mx} \ell=44$ Magnetic moment of arc $\begin{aligned}& =\mathrm{m} \times 2 \mathrm{r} \\& =\mathrm{m} \times \frac{2 \ell}{\pi} \\& =\frac{44 \times 2}{\pi}=\frac{88}{\pi}=28\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two parallel, long wires are kept <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart in vacuum, each carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the same direction. If the force of attraction per meter of each wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is approximately
question: Two parallel, long wires are kept <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart in vacuum, each carrying current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the same direction. If the force of attraction per meter of each wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is approximately
solution:
Force per unit length between two parallel wires is Fl=μ0i1i22πd

Here, i1=i2=i and Fl=2×10-6 N

Then, 2×10-6=4π2×10-7i22π×0.2

⇒i2=2⇒i=1.4 A

Hence, x=1.4.

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, a current of 2 A flowing in an equilateral triangle of side 43 cm . The magnetic field at the centroid O of the triangle is :

(Neglect the effect of earth’s magnetic field)
question:
As shown in the figure, a current of 2 A flowing in an equilateral triangle of side 43 cm . The magnetic field at the centroid O of the triangle is :

(Neglect the effect of earth’s magnetic field)
solution:
Perpendicular distance of centroid from any of the sides will be, dtan60°=23

⇒d=2 cm

Each side will subtend an angle of 120° at the centre. Also magnetic field due to all sides will be out of plane of paper, hence total magnetic field can be written as

B=3×μ0i4πdsin60°+sin60°

=3×10-7×22×10-2×3

=33×10-5 T

subject: Physics
q: A triangular shaped wire carrying 10 A current is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T, as shown in figure. The magnetic force on segment CD is (Given BC=CD=BD=5 cm).
question: A triangular shaped wire carrying 10 A current is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T, as shown in figure. The magnetic force on segment CD is (Given BC=CD=BD=5 cm).
solution:
Force due to magnetic field on a current carrying conductor is given by, F→=il→×B→.

The angle between the magnetic field and the direction of the current in the wire CD is 60°. Therefore,

F=ilBsin60°⇒F=10×0.05×0.5×32⇒F=0.216 N

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> are the magnetic field at the centre of two coils of two coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> respectively, each carrying equal current. If coil <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> turns and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> radius and coil <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn></math> turns and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> radius, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> are the magnetic field at the centre of two coils of two coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> respectively, each carrying equal current. If coil <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> turns and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> radius and coil <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn></math> turns and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> radius, the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> is
solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of a single coil is B=μ0i2R

If N number of loops are kept closely B=Nμ0i2R

Therefore,

BxBy=NxNy

⇒BxBy=12

subject: Physics
q: A 2 A current carrying straight metal wire of resistance $1 \Omega$, resistivity $2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega \mathrm{m}$, area of cross-section $10 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ and mass $500 \mathrm{~g}$ is suspended horizontally in mid air by applying a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. The magnitude of $B$ is _____$\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~T}$ (given, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
question: A 2 A current carrying straight metal wire of resistance $1 \Omega$, resistivity $2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega \mathrm{m}$, area of cross-section $10 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ and mass $500 \mathrm{~g}$ is suspended horizontally in mid air by applying a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. The magnitude of $B$ is _____$\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~T}$ (given, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}} \Rightarrow \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times \ell}{10^{-5}}=1 \Rightarrow \ell=5 \\ & \mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{Bi} \ell\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{i} \ell}=\frac{5}{2 \times 5}=0.5=5 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{Tesla}$

q: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> . At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> , a positively charged particle is at the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . If its instantaneous velocity at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> , the force acting on it due to the wave is:
question: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> . At <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> , a positively charged particle is at the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . If its instantaneous velocity at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> , the force acting on it due to the wave is:
solution:
Force due to electric field is in direction –i^+j^2E0
Force due to magnetic filed is in direction qv→×B→ and v→||k^
∴ it is parallel to E→
∴ net force is antiparallel to i^+j^2 .

subject: Physics
q: A coil having 100 turns, area of $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$, carrying current of $1 \mathrm{~mA}$ is placed in uniform magnetic field of $0.20 \mathrm{~T}$ such a way that plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in turning the coil through $90^{\circ}$ is _______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
question: A coil having 100 turns, area of $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$, carrying current of $1 \mathrm{~mA}$ is placed in uniform magnetic field of $0.20 \mathrm{~T}$ such a way that plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in turning the coil through $90^{\circ}$ is _______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & W=\Delta U=U_f-U_i \\ & W=(-\vec{\mu} . \vec{B})_f-(-\vec{\mu} . \vec{B})_i \\ & =0+(\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B})_i \\ & =\left(100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 0.2 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}=100 \mu \mathrm{J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Non-polar materials do not have any permanent dipole moment.

Reason (R): When a non-polar material is placed in an electric field, the centre of the positive charge distribution of it's individual atom or molecule coincides with the centre of the negative charge distribution.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Non-polar materials do not have any permanent dipole moment.

Reason (R): When a non-polar material is placed in an electric field, the centre of the positive charge distribution of it's individual atom or molecule coincides with the centre of the negative charge distribution.

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct.
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct.
solution:
Non-polar materials do not have any permanent dipole moment. This statement is true.

When a non-polar material is placed in an electric field, centre of positive charge is pushed in the direction of the electric field and centre of the negative charge is pushed in the direction opposite to the electric field. Hence, they don't coincide. Therefore, reason is false.

q: Proton, Deuteron and alpha particle of the same kinetic energy are moving in circular trajectories in a constant magnetic field. The radii of proton, deuteron and alpha particle are respectively $r_p, r_d$ and $r_\alpha$. Which one of the following relations is correct?
question: Proton, Deuteron and alpha particle of the same kinetic energy are moving in circular trajectories in a constant magnetic field. The radii of proton, deuteron and alpha particle are respectively $r_p, r_d$ and $r_\alpha$. Which one of the following relations is correct?
solution:
$r=\frac{\sqrt{2 m K}}{B q}$
$r \propto \frac{\sqrt{m}}{q}$
$r_\alpha=r_p < r_d$

q:
A rectangular loop of sides 10 cm and 5 cm, carrying a current I of 12 A, is placed in different orientations as shown in the figure below.
(a)  (b)

(c)  (d)

If there is a uniform magnetic field of 0.3 T in the positive z direction, in which orientations the loop would be in (i) stable equilibrium and (ii) unstable equilibrium?
question:
A rectangular loop of sides 10 cm and 5 cm, carrying a current I of 12 A, is placed in different orientations as shown in the figure below.
(a)  (b)

(c)  (d)

If there is a uniform magnetic field of 0.3 T in the positive z direction, in which orientations the loop would be in (i) stable equilibrium and (ii) unstable equilibrium?
solution:
For a magnetic dipole placed in a uniform magnetic field, the torque is given by τ→=M→×B→, and potential energy U is given as,

U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

When M→ is in the same direction as B→ then τ→=0 and U is minimum = -MB as θ=0o.⇒ Stable equilibrium is (b).

When M→ the opposite direction of B→, then τ→=0 and U is max = +MB
As, θ=180o ⇒Unstable equilibrium in (d)

q: Two long parallel wires $X$ and $Y$, separated by a distance of 6 cm , carry currents of 5A and 4A, respectively, in opposite directions as shown in the figure. Magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at point P at a distance of 4 cm from wire Y is $x \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $x$ is__________ . Take permeability of free space as $\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{SI}$ units.
question: Two long parallel wires $X$ and $Y$, separated by a distance of 6 cm , carry currents of 5A and 4A, respectively, in opposite directions as shown in the figure. Magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at point P at a distance of 4 cm from wire Y is $x \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $x$ is__________ . Take permeability of free space as $\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{SI}$ units.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & B=\frac{\mu_0(5)}{2 \pi \times .01}-\frac{\mu_0 4}{2 \pi \times 0.04} \\ & =-\frac{100 \mu_0}{4 \pi} \\ & =-100 \times 10^{-7} \\ & =-1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}\end{aligned}$

q: Two ions having same mass have charges in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>. question: Two ions having same mass have charges in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>. They are projected normally in a uniform magnetic field with their speeds in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></math>. The ratio of the radii of their circular trajectories is,
solution:
R=mvqB⇒R1R2=mv1q1 Bmv2q2 B=v1q1×q2v2=q2q1×v1v2

=21×23=43

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The loop is placed in uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The plane of loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in pulling the loop out of the magnetic field slowly and uniformly in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> sec, will be
question: A square loop of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The loop is placed in uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The plane of loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in pulling the loop out of the magnetic field slowly and uniformly in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> sec, will be
solution:
As l2=25 cm2, then l=5 cm=0.05 m.

Given: t=1 s

Velocity of the square loop, v=0.051=0.05 m s-1.

Induced current,

i=VR=BlvR=40×0.05×0.0510=0.01 A

Now force acting on the side of the square loop, F=Bil=40×0.01×0.05

⇒F=0.02 N

Therefore, work done

W=Fl=0.02×l=0.02×0.05

⇒W=1×10-3 J

subject: Physics
q: Two insulated circular loop A and B radius a carrying a current of I in the anti clockwise direction as shown in figure. The magnitude of the magnetic induction at the centre will be:
question: Two insulated circular loop A and B radius a carrying a current of I in the anti clockwise direction as shown in figure. The magnitude of the magnetic induction at the centre will be:
solution:
Right hand thumb rule states that, “If you imagine holding a current carrying wire in your right-hand with your thumb pointing towards the direction of electric current flow then the direction in which your fingers curl, gives the direction of lines of force of the magnetic field”. In this case, direciton of magnetic field at the centre for both wires is as shown below.

Magnetic field due to a circular wire is given by, BA=BB=μ0I2a

As both magnetic field lines at centre are perpendicular, therefore

∵Bnet=BA2+BB2=2μ0I2a=μ0I2a

question: An infinitely long current carrying wire and a small current carrying loop are in the plane of the paper as shown. The radius of the loop is a and distance of its centre from the wire is d d≫a. If the loop applies a force F on the wire then:
solution:

F=i2πa2×ddrμ0i12πr

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic intensity at the centre of a long current carrying solenoid is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the number of turns is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>, then the current flowing through the solenoid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: The magnetic intensity at the centre of a long current carrying solenoid is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the number of turns is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>, then the current flowing through the solenoid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
The magnetic intensity H is defined as the product of the number of turns per unit length in a coil n and the current that it carries I.

The magnetic field of a solenoid is given by B=μ0ni

H=Bμ0=μ0niμ0⇒H=ni   ...(i) 

The given data is 

H=1.6×103 A m-1

n=8 per cm

Substituting the values in equation (i)

⇒1.6×1000=81100×i

⇒i=2 A

subject: Physics
q:
In a moving coil galvanometer, two moving coils $M_1$ and $M_2$ have the following particulars :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}_1=5 \Omega, \mathrm{~N}_1=15, \mathrm{~A}_1=3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{~B}_1=0.25 \mathrm{~T} \\ & \mathrm{R}_2=7 \Omega, \mathrm{~N}_2=21, \mathrm{~A}_2=1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{~B}_2=0.50 \mathrm{~T}
\end{aligned}$
Assuming that torsional constant of the springs are same for both coils, what will be the ratio of voltage sensitivity of $M_1$ and $M_2$ ?
question:
In a moving coil galvanometer, two moving coils $M_1$ and $M_2$ have the following particulars :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}_1=5 \Omega, \mathrm{~N}_1=15, \mathrm{~A}_1=3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{~B}_1=0.25 \mathrm{~T} \\ & \mathrm{R}_2=7 \Omega, \mathrm{~N}_2=21, \mathrm{~A}_2=1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{~B}_2=0.50 \mathrm{~T}
\end{aligned}$
Assuming that torsional constant of the springs are same for both coils, what will be the ratio of voltage sensitivity of $M_1$ and $M_2$ ?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Voltage sensitivity }=\frac{\theta}{V}=\frac{N A B}{c R} \\ & \text { Ratio }==\left(\frac{\mathrm{N}_1 \mathrm{~A}_1 \mathrm{~B}_1}{\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{~A}_2 \mathrm{~B}_2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{15 \times 3.6 \times 0.25}{21 \times 1.8 \times 0.5} \times \frac{7}{5}=\frac{1}{1}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If the number of turns in the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is doubled then the current sensitivity becomes double.

Statement II: Increasing current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer by only increasing the number of turns in the coil will also increase its voltage sensitivity in the same ratio

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: If the number of turns in the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is doubled then the current sensitivity becomes double.

Statement II: Increasing current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer by only increasing the number of turns in the coil will also increase its voltage sensitivity in the same ratio

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
solution:
For a moving coil galvanometer, 

NiAB=kθ

The current sensitivity is given by 

Si=NABk

So, if N is doubled then, the current sensitivity is also doubled.

The voltage is given by 

V=BNARkθ

The voltage sensitivity is given by

Sv=NABkR

As, N is increased, R also increases. So, the voltage sensitivity does not change as NR remains constant.

subject: Physics
q: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2 \pi \mathrm{cm}$ are placed at right angles to each other. 3 Ampere and 4 ampere are the currents flowing in each coil respectively. The magnetic induction in Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$ at the centre of the coils will be $\left(\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right.$ Wb/A-m)
question: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2 \pi \mathrm{cm}$ are placed at right angles to each other. 3 Ampere and 4 ampere are the currents flowing in each coil respectively. The magnetic induction in Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$ at the centre of the coils will be $\left(\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right.$ Wb/A-m)
solution:
$B=\frac{\mu_o}{2 r} \sqrt{I_1^2+I_2^2}$
$B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 2 \pi \times 10^{-2}} \times 5$
$B=5 \times 10^{-5}$

subject: Physics
q:
A charged particle of mass ‘ m ’ and charge ‘ q ’ moving under the influence of uniform electric field E →i^ and a uniform magnetic field B →k^ follows a trajectory from point P to Q as shown in figure. The velocities at P and Q are respectively, vi→ and -2vj→ . Then which of the following statements (A, B, C, D) are the correct? (Trajectory shown is schematic and not to scale)



A E=32mv2qa
B Rate of work done by the electric field at P is 32mv3a
C Rate of work done by both the fields at Q is zero
D The difference between the magnitude of angular momentum of the particle at P and Q is 2mav .
question:
A charged particle of mass ‘ m ’ and charge ‘ q ’ moving under the influence of uniform electric field E →i^ and a uniform magnetic field B →k^ follows a trajectory from point P to Q as shown in figure. The velocities at P and Q are respectively, vi→ and -2vj→ . Then which of the following statements (A, B, C, D) are the correct? (Trajectory shown is schematic and not to scale)



A E=32mv2qa
B Rate of work done by the electric field at P is 32mv3a
C Rate of work done by both the fields at Q is zero
D The difference between the magnitude of angular momentum of the particle at P and Q is 2mav .
solution:
(A) by work energy theorem

Wmag+Wele=12m(2v)2-12m(v)2

0+qE02a=32mv2
E0=34mv2qa

(B) Rate of work done at A= power of electric force

=qE0V
=34mv3a

(C) at Q,dwdt=0 for both forces

D ΔL→=(-m2 v 2ak^)-(- mvak )

|ΔL→|=3mva

q: A thin ring of <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> radius carries a uniformly distributed charge. The ring rotates at a constant angular speed of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> about its axis, perpendicular to its plane. Is the magnetic field its centre is <math><mn>3.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> , then the charge carried by the ring is close to <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A thin ring of <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> radius carries a uniformly distributed charge. The ring rotates at a constant angular speed of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> about its axis, perpendicular to its plane. Is the magnetic field its centre is <math><mn>3.8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> , then the charge carried by the ring is close to <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
i=qt=q2π/ω=qω2π
B=μ0i2R=μ0qω4πR
4π×10-7×q×40π4π×(0.1)=3.8×10-9
q×400π=3.8×10-2
q=3×10-5 C

subject: Physics
q:
A rod with circular cross-section area 2 cm2 and length 40 cm is wound uniformly with 400 turns of an insulated wire. If a current of 0.4 A flows in the wire windings, the total magnetic flux produced inside windings is 4π×10-6 Wb. The relative permeability of the rod is

(Given : Permeability of vacuum μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2)
question:
A rod with circular cross-section area 2 cm2 and length 40 cm is wound uniformly with 400 turns of an insulated wire. If a current of 0.4 A flows in the wire windings, the total magnetic flux produced inside windings is 4π×10-6 Wb. The relative permeability of the rod is

(Given : Permeability of vacuum μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2)
solution:
Magnetic flux is given by ϕ=NBA, here, N is number of turns, B is magnetic field and A is area.

Given here, ϕ=4π×10-6 Wb, A=2 cm2, N=400, I=0.4 A.

Putting the values, 

ϕ=400×μrμ0nI×A

⇒4π×10-6 =400μr×4π×10-7×4000.4×0.4×2×10-4

⇒μr=125

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic moment of an electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>e</mi></mfenced></math> revolving in an orbit around nucleus with an orbital angular momentum is given by
question: The magnetic moment of an electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>e</mi></mfenced></math> revolving in an orbit around nucleus with an orbital angular momentum is given by
solution:
When a charged body is rotating in a circle, the ratio of magnetic moment and angular momentum is given by,

μ→L→=q2m

The charge of electron q=-e

μ→=-e2mL→

subject: Physics
q: The figure shows a region of length 'l' with a uniform magnetic field of  0.3 T in it and a proton entering the region with velocity 4×105 m s-1 making an angle 60° with the field. If the proton completes 10 revolution by the time it cross the region shown, 'l' is close to (mass of proton = 1.67×10-27 kg, charge of the proton= 1.6×10-19 C)
question: The figure shows a region of length 'l' with a uniform magnetic field of  0.3 T in it and a proton entering the region with velocity 4×105 m s-1 making an angle 60° with the field. If the proton completes 10 revolution by the time it cross the region shown, 'l' is close to (mass of proton = 1.67×10-27 kg, charge of the proton= 1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
ℓ=10×pitch

=10×vcos60°×2πmgB

ℓ=10πmvqB
Put in the value of given data we find ℓ=0.44 m
